fixes.description: Let the unit complete the cool-down — the door will unlock when it's done.

error_codes.reset_instructions: No action needed. Cd clears automatically when the tub cools.
error_codes.models_affected: LG washer-dryer combo units
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Cd is not a fault. No service required.

error_codes.meaning: Over-current error — motor drawing too much current
error_codes.detail: CE fires when the motor control board reads current above its safe threshold on the direct-drive motor circuit. The board shuts down mid-cycle to protect the motor and electronics. Because LG's stator uses a hall sensor to report rotor position and speed, a faulty sensor sends bad feedback that the board misreads as an over-current condition. This distinguishes CE from LE (locked/overloaded motor) and SE (stator/sensor communication), though overloading and winding faults can also trigger it.

causes.description: Hall sensor failure causing incorrect current feedback

fixes.title: Reduce load
fixes.description: Remove items and restart with half load.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Unplug 10 minutes.

fixes.title: Replace hall sensor
fixes.description: The hall sensor is the $15–$30 fix that resolves CE in most cases on LG direct-drive washers.
fixes.warning: Unplug and lay washer on front to access motor.

fixes.title: Test and replace motor if needed
fixes.description: Test stator winding resistance if hall sensor replacement doesn't help.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug 10 minutes, reduce load, restart.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If hall sensor replacement doesn't resolve CE, the motor or control board needs professional testing.
error_codes.models_affected: LG direct-drive front-load and top-load washers, including models such as WM3900HWA, WM4000HBA, WM4500HBA, WM3400CW, WT7300CW, WT7900HBA, and WM8100HVA
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report CE appearing during the spin or high-speed phase, often after adding a heavy or unbalanced load. A false trigger from an oversized wash is frequent, so many find that splitting the load and power-cycling clears it; when it keeps returning, the inexpensive hall sensor is the fix people most often mention.

error_codes.meaning: Hall sensor error — motor speed sensor fault
error_codes.detail: The hall sensor measuring motor rotation speed has failed. The motor control board cannot safely spin the drum without accurate speed feedback.

fixes.title: Replace the hall sensor
fixes.description: The most cost-effective repair for SE on LG direct-drive washers.
fixes.warning: Lay washer on front — motor is at the back.

fixes.title: Check 3-wire harness
fixes.description: Inspect the 3-wire connector from hall sensor to control board.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After hall sensor replacement, unplug 1 minute and restart.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If new hall sensor and error persists, control board hall sensor input circuit has failed.

error_codes.meaning: Water level pressure sensor error
error_codes.detail: PE fires when the control board receives an out-of-range or non-changing signal from the water-level pressure sensor during fill. The sensor reads air pressure in a small hose connected to the tub, translating water height into a frequency the board monitors. If that signal is implausible or absent, the board halts filling to prevent overfill or dry running. Unlike FE (overflow) or IE (slow/no inlet water), PE points specifically to the sensing circuit rather than actual water flow.

fixes.title: Check pressure hose
fixes.description: Locate the small hose running from the tub to the pressure sensor. Check for kinks or disconnection.

fixes.title: Blow test the sensor
fixes.description: Disconnect the hose from the sensor. Gently blow — you should hear audible clicks.

fixes.title: Replace pressure sensor
fixes.description: If sensor doesn't click, replace it.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug the washer for 60 seconds, then restart and let it fill. If PE returns during fill, recheck that the pressure hose is fully seated on the sensor and free of kinks before replacing parts.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If new sensor and clear hose don't resolve PE, the control board pressure input has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG top-load and front-load washers using an analog water-level pressure sensor, including models such as the WM3900HWA, WM4000HBA, WM3400CW, WT7300CW, WM8100HVA, WM4500HBA, and WT7900HBA
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report PE appearing at the start of a cycle before much water enters, and a pinched, cracked, or slipped-off pressure hose is a frequent false trigger that mimics a dead sensor. Reseating the hose and clearing any kinks often clears it without buying parts.

error_codes.meaning: EEPROM error — control board memory failure
error_codes.detail: EE indicates the main control board cannot reliably read from or write to its onboard EEPROM, the non-volatile memory holding model configuration, cycle history, and calibration values. When a checksum or read/write verification fails, the board flags EE and may halt, restart, or throw erratic secondary errors. Unlike sensor codes tied to a single component, EE points at the board's own memory, so it usually persists after resets and is not corrected by servicing motors, valves, or drain parts.

causes.description: Voltage spike or outage corrupted memory

fixes.title: Power cycle the washer
fixes.description: Unplug for 15 minutes so the control fully discharges, then restart and watch whether EE returns.

fixes.title: Check control connectors
fixes.description: With power off, reseat the harnesses at the main board; intermittent power to the board can mimic memory faults.
fixes.warning: Unplug the washer before accessing the control board.

fixes.title: Replace the main control board
fixes.description: If EE returns after a long reset, replace the control board because the EEPROM is not serviceable separately.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug the washer for 15 minutes to let the board fully discharge, then restore power and start a simple rinse/spin cycle; if EE returns, the EEPROM fault is stored in the main control board and will not be fixed by more resets.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Call a technician if EE returns after the long power reset or after reseating the board connectors, because the repair requires matching and replacing the correct main control board rather than swapping a separate memory chip.
error_codes.models_affected: LG top-load and front-load washers with electronic control boards; the fault is board-level and not tied to a specific model, though it can appear on any current LG washer
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report EE appearing suddenly after a storm, brownout, or power flicker, and a long unplug sometimes clears a one-off memory glitch. When it returns after that full discharge, it usually signals a genuine board fault rather than a false trigger.

error_codes.meaning: Clutch motor error (top-load models)
error_codes.detail: E6 fires when the control board commands the clutch actuator to switch the drum between agitation and spin, but the expected mechanical engagement or actuator feedback never occurs. The board interprets the missing response as a failed clutch motor, actuator switch, or broken wiring in that circuit. Unlike LE, which points to a stalled or locked drive motor, E6 isolates the clutch mechanism responsible for mode changes rather than the main drive itself.

fixes.title: Access the clutch motor
fixes.description: On top-load LG models, the clutch motor is beneath the drum.
fixes.warning: Unplug and lay machine on its side.

fixes.title: Test clutch motor
fixes.description: Apply 12V — should run freely.

fixes.title: Replace clutch assembly
fixes.description: If motor or switch has failed.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug the washer for 60 seconds, then plug back in and start a Rinse & Spin cycle. If E6 returns as soon as the drum tries to switch between agitate and spin, the clutch hardware fault is confirmed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG top-load washers with a clutch-driven drum, including WT-series models such as WT7300CW and WT7900HBA
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Clutch motor replacement requires significant disassembly of top-load models.
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report E6 appearing right at the transition from wash to spin, or during a Rinse & Spin, often with the tub failing to engage properly. A loose or corroded connector at the clutch motor is sometimes mistaken for a dead clutch, so reseating the wiring before ordering parts can occasionally clear it.

error_codes.meaning: Button error — control panel key stuck
error_codes.detail: The bE code fires when the control board detects a single panel button registering a continuous press longer than the firmware allows during power-up or standby. To prevent an unintended cycle, it locks the keypad and blocks start. Unlike a full UI communication fault, bE points specifically at the button-sensing layer — a mechanically stuck key, moisture bridging a membrane contact, or a shorted keypad trace holding one input active rather than a dead or unresponsive board.

fixes.title: Check for stuck button
fixes.description: Press each button and check for sticky feel.

fixes.title: Clean control panel
fixes.description: Wipe with dry cloth around all buttons.

fixes.title: Replace UI board
fixes.description: If button remains stuck.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug 5 minutes. Clear stuck button before restart.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If UI board replacement doesn't fix bE, main board button interface has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG top-load and front-load washers with membrane-style touch control panels, including models such as the WM3900HWA, WM4000HBA, WM3400CW, WT7300CW, and WT7900HBA
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report bE appearing after spills or humid laundry-room conditions, and it often clears once the panel is wiped dry and each button is worked loose. A quick unplug-and-clean frequently resolves it before any part replacement is needed.

error_codes.meaning: Noise sensor error (front-load models)
error_codes.detail: The nE code fires when the control board loses a valid signal from the noise sensor, a component that detects vibration and mechanical noise to help gauge load imbalance and modulate spin speed. The board flags an out-of-range or absent sensor reading, most often from a failed sensor, a loose connector near the motor, or a bad sensor input on the board. Unlike unbalance codes (UE) that stop the drum, nE reflects a sensor fault rather than an actual load-distribution problem.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Unplug 5 minutes and restart.

fixes.title: Check sensor connector
fixes.description: Locate noise sensor (usually near the motor) and reseat connector.

fixes.title: Replace noise sensor
fixes.description: If error persists.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug the washer for 5 minutes to reset the control board, then restart the cycle. If nE reappears, reseat the noise sensor connector near the motor before retrying — a loose plug often clears it.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: nE rarely prevents normal washing — cycle may complete with reduced spin speed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG front-load washers equipped with a noise/vibration sensor for imbalance detection (this code does not apply to top-load models like the WT-series)
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report that nE appears at the start of or during the spin phase, and that cycles still finish, sometimes with a noticeably gentler spin. A simple power cycle or reseating the sensor connector near the motor often clears what turns out to be a false trigger from a loose plug.

error_codes.meaning: Duct blockage detected — exhaust duct 80% blocked
error_codes.detail: D80 is a Flow Sense diagnostic reading, not a hard fault. LG's control board monitors airflow and back-pressure through the exhaust; when it calculates roughly 80% restriction, it displays D80 as a warning while the cycle continues. It reflects reduced airflow from accumulated lint or duct issues rather than a component failure. It differs from D90 and D95, which indicate progressively worse blockage (about 90% and 95%) and greater overheating risk.

causes.description: Lint buildup in exhaust duct over time
Kinked or crushed flexible duct behind dryer
Exterior vent cap clogged or flap stuck closed
Duct run too long or too many 90° bends

fixes.title: Clean the lint filter
fixes.description: Remove the lint filter (inside the door) and clean thoroughly. This should be done after every cycle.

fixes.title: Disconnect and clean the exhaust duct
fixes.description: Pull the dryer from the wall and disconnect the duct from the back. Use a dryer vent brush kit to clean the full length of the duct from both ends.

fixes.title: Check the exterior vent
fixes.description: Go outside and locate the exhaust vent. Remove any lint buildup, bird nests, or debris. Ensure the flap opens freely.

fixes.title: Replace flexible duct with rigid metal
fixes.description: Flexible foil or plastic ducts crush easily and trap lint in their ridges. Replace with smooth rigid metal duct for runs over 3 feet.

fixes.title: Recheck duct routing
fixes.description: Maximum recommended duct run: 25 feet with no more than two 90° elbows. Reduce run length if possible.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After clearing the duct, run an Air Only (no heat) cycle for 20 minutes. The D80 code will clear once the Flow Sense sensor detects adequate airflow. If it doesn't clear after a full cycle, blockage remains.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If the duct is completely clean and the code persists, the Flow Sense sensor itself may have failed. Professional diagnosis needed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dryers equipped with the Flow Sense duct-monitoring system, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report D80 appearing partway through or at the end of a cycle, often after skipped duct cleanings, and it sometimes clears simply by emptying a full lint filter or straightening a kinked hose behind the unit. A stuck exterior vent flap or a bird nest can trigger it even when the indoor duct looks clean.

error_codes.meaning: Duct blockage detected — exhaust duct 90% blocked
error_codes.detail: The D90 code is generated by LG's Flow Sense system, which uses a duct sensor to gauge restriction in the exhaust path. When the control board calculates roughly 90% blockage, it interprets airflow as dangerously low and cuts or reduces heat to avoid overheating. Unlike D80, which warns of moderate restriction while allowing continued drying, D90 signals a critical condition. It differs from a heating or thermistor fault because the trigger is airflow-based, not element-based.

causes.description: Severe lint buildup in exhaust duct
Multiple blockages: lint filter + duct + exterior cap
Duct too long or too many bends causing low flow

fixes.title: Stop using the dryer immediately
fixes.description: A 90% blockage is a fire risk. Do not continue using the dryer until the duct is cleared.
fixes.warning: Fire hazard — stop all drying until resolved.

fixes.title: Deep-clean the entire duct system
fixes.description: Clean the lint filter, the full length of the exhaust duct, the connection point at the dryer, and the exterior vent cap. Use a professional-length dryer vent brush.

fixes.title: Check for duct damage
fixes.description: If the duct is made of flexible plastic or foil, it may have crushed inside a wall. Replace with rigid metal duct. Consider hiring a duct cleaning service for in-wall runs.

fixes.title: Test airflow manually
fixes.description: After cleaning, hold your hand at the exterior vent while the dryer runs on Air Only. You should feel strong airflow.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After completely clearing all blockages, run the dryer on Air Only for 20–30 minutes. The D90 code will clear once adequate airflow is confirmed.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If duct cleaning doesn't clear the code, the Flow Sense sensor may have failed, or you may need a professional duct cleaning service for complex wall-routed duct runs.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dryers equipped with the Flow Sense duct-monitoring system, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report D90 appearing gradually after months of skipped duct cleaning, often alongside longer cycles and hot, damp laundry. A clogged lint filter, a snow- or debris-blocked exterior vent cap, or a crushed flexible duct behind the dryer can falsely inflate the reading. The frequent quick win is a full duct-to-cap cleaning, after which an Air Only run clears the code.

error_codes.meaning: Duct blockage detected — exhaust duct 95% blocked
error_codes.detail: D95 is the most severe tier of LG's FlowSense airflow monitoring. During a cycle, sensors compare exhaust temperature and airflow against expected values; when restriction reaches roughly 95%, the control board flags near-total blockage and often halts the cycle to prevent overheating. Unlike D80 (80%) and D90 (90%), which are early warnings that still allow drying, D95 indicates airflow is critically compromised and the dryer treats continued operation as a fire risk.

causes.description: Duct physically collapsed or crushed inside wall

fixes.title: Stop using the dryer immediately
fixes.description: 95% blockage is a fire emergency. Do not run the dryer.
fixes.warning: FIRE HAZARD — do not operate until fully resolved.

fixes.title: Call a professional duct cleaning service
fixes.description: At 95% blockage, the duct may have compacted lint that a brush alone won't clear. A professional service uses high-powered vacuum equipment.

fixes.title: Inspect the duct in the wall
fixes.description: If the duct runs inside walls, there may be a physical collapse. You may need to replace the entire run.

error_codes.reset_instructions: The D95 code will clear only after a thorough, professional-level duct cleaning. Run Air Only cycle to confirm airflow is restored.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: At 95% blockage, a professional duct cleaning service is strongly recommended. DIY cleaning may not be sufficient for compacted lint deep in the duct.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dryers with the FlowSense duct-monitoring feature, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report D95 appearing after long-neglected duct maintenance or when clothes stop drying entirely, sometimes escalating from earlier D80/D90 warnings. A crushed or disconnected vent behind the dryer, or a blocked exterior wall flap, can occasionally trigger it falsely, so checking the full run end-to-end often resolves it faster than expected.

error_codes.meaning: Thermistor failure — temperature sensor not reading
error_codes.detail: F9 (also shown as E1) fires when the control board reads the thermistor's resistance as out of range — typically an open circuit, a short, or a value that never changes as the drum heats. Because the board relies on this sensor to regulate heat safely, it halts or limits heating rather than risk overheating. Unlike a clogged-vent or high-limit fault, F9 points to the sensor's electrical reading itself, not to actual excessive temperature or airflow restriction.

fixes.title: Test the thermistor
fixes.description: The thermistor is usually on the exhaust duct inside the dryer. At room temp, test resistance: ~10k ohms expected.

fixes.title: Check connector
fixes.description: Reseat the connector at the thermistor.

fixes.title: Replace thermistor
fixes.description: A simple pull-and-click replacement if the sensor is accessible.
fixes.warning: Unplug dryer first.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After repair, unplug for 1 minute then restart. Code clears after a successful cycle.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If thermistor is new and error continues, the control board thermistor input is faulty.
error_codes.models_affected: LG electric and gas dryers with a duct-mounted thermistor, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report this code appearing at the start of a cycle or shortly after heating begins, sometimes with clothes coming out damp. A loose or corroded connector at the sensor is a frequent false trigger, and reseating it is the quick win many people mention before replacing the part.

error_codes.meaning: Child lock is active — controls are locked
error_codes.detail: CL is a status indicator rather than a fault: it signals that the Child Lock mode is engaged, so the control board ignores all touch or button inputs except Power. It fires whenever the lock is toggled on, either deliberately or by accidentally holding the assigned button. Unlike genuine error codes, no sensor fault or component failure is involved, which is why it clears simply by disabling the lock rather than through diagnostics or repair.

fixes.title: Disable child lock
fixes.description: Press and hold the Child Lock button for 3–5 seconds until the CL indicator turns off. On some models, press and hold 'Temp' for 3 seconds.

fixes.title: Power cycle if a button is stuck
fixes.description: If CL won't clear, unplug the dryer for 60 seconds, plug back in, and hold the Child Lock button again. A sticky button may need cleaning around its edges.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Hold the Child Lock button for 3 seconds to unlock.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Child lock does not require service.
error_codes.models_affected: Most LG electric and gas dryers with an electronic control panel, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report CL appearing after cleaning the panel or when children press buttons, and are surprised the dryer won't start. The quick win is usually holding the Child Lock (or Temp) button for a few seconds until the indicator disappears; a brief power cycle tends to resolve the rare case where a sticky button keeps re-triggering it.

error_codes.meaning: No power to the heating element
error_codes.detail: The nP code fires when the control board confirms the dryer is running but senses no voltage reaching the heating element. Because electric dryers draw 240V across two breaker legs, losing one leg lets the 120V motor turn while the element stays cold, triggering the code. The board can't distinguish a tripped half-breaker from an open element, burned connector, or failed heater relay, so it reports the shared symptom rather than a specific component fault.

causes.description: Tripped circuit breaker on the dryer's 240V circuit
Failed heater relay on main control board
Burned wire or connector to heating element

fixes.title: Check the circuit breaker
fixes.description: Electric dryers use a 240V circuit with two breaker switches. If one side trips, the dryer runs (motor works on 120V) but won't heat. Reset both breaker switches.

fixes.title: Inspect the heating element wiring
fixes.description: Check the wiring terminals at the heating element for burn marks or loose connections.

fixes.title: Test the heating element
fixes.description: Disconnect and check for continuity. Should read approximately 10–15 ohms.

fixes.title: Check control board relay
fixes.description: If element and wiring are good, the relay that controls element power has failed.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Reset the circuit breaker first. If that doesn't resolve it, unplug dryer for 5 minutes and restart.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Electrical faults (heater relays, control boards) require professional diagnosis if basic checks don't resolve the issue.
error_codes.models_affected: Electric LG dryers with a 240V heating circuit, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, and DLE7150W (gas models like DLG3601W heat differently and rarely display this code)
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report this appearing as clothes coming out damp despite the drum tumbling normally, and the most frequent quick win is resetting both switches of the double breaker since one side can trip silently. A partially seated or storm-affected breaker is a common false trigger before any real component has failed.

error_codes.meaning: Exhaust duct ~75% blocked — airflow restriction warning
error_codes.detail: D75 comes from LG's FlowSense system, which uses an airflow/pressure sensor in the exhaust path to gauge how freely air moves out of the drum. When it detects resistance consistent with roughly 75% blockage, it posts this warning rather than a hard fault, so cycles still run but slower and hotter. It's the entry point of the warning tier — distinct from d80/d90/d95, which fire at progressively worse restriction, and from heater or thermistor faults that address temperature rather than airflow.

causes.description: Crushed or kinked flexible transition hose

fixes.title: Clean the lint filter
fixes.description: Remove and clean the lint screen before every load. A clogged screen alone can trigger D75.

fixes.title: Clear the full exhaust duct
fixes.description: Disconnect the duct and clean its entire length plus the exterior vent cap with a vent brush.

fixes.title: Check the transition hose
fixes.description: Replace any crushed or kinked flexible hose behind the dryer with rigid or semi-rigid duct.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Clean the lint filter and exhaust duct, then restart. D75 clears once airflow is restored.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If the duct and vent are verified clear but D75 returns, the flow sensor or blower wheel may need inspection.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dryers with the FlowSense airflow-monitoring feature, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report D75 appearing after several months without a vent cleaning, or on longer duct runs and exterior caps clogged with lint or debris. A frequent false trigger is simply a full lint screen, so many find that cleaning the filter and clearing the outside vent flap makes the code disappear quickly.

error_codes.meaning: Power supply error — improper voltage at the cord or terminal block
error_codes.detail: The control monitors incoming line voltage across both hot legs of the 240V supply. PS fires when it detects an out-of-range or absent voltage condition — most often a power cord wired incorrectly, a loose or burnt terminal-block screw, or one hot leg missing from a half-tripped double-pole breaker or a faulty outlet. Unlike nP (no power/plug detection), PS indicates the dryer is receiving power but the voltage present is improper or incomplete for normal operation.

causes.description: Power cord miswired or loose at terminal block

fixes.title: Check the breaker
fixes.description: Confirm BOTH halves of the dryer's double-pole breaker are on. A half-tripped breaker leaves one leg dead.

fixes.title: Inspect the cord and terminal block
fixes.description: Unplug, remove the terminal cover, and check that the cord wires are tight and undamaged on the block.
fixes.warning: Unplug the dryer before removing the terminal block cover.

fixes.title: Verify outlet voltage
fixes.description: Have an electrician confirm 240V across the legs and 120V each leg to ground at the outlet.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Reset both halves of the breaker and confirm the cord is tight at the terminal block, then restart.
error_codes.models_affected: LG electric dryers with 240V terminal-block wiring
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Open wiring inside the wall outlet or a damaged terminal block requires a licensed electrician.
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report PS right after installation or after moving the dryer, which points to a miswired or loose cord at the terminal block. It can also appear seemingly at random when one side of the breaker trips, and many find a quick win by simply cycling both halves of the double-pole breaker fully off then on.

error_codes.meaning: Door error — dryer door not fully closed
error_codes.detail: The control monitors the door switch circuit and expects continuity confirming the door is latched before enabling the drum motor and heat. When it doesn't see that closed-loop signal, it displays dE and blocks the cycle from starting. Most often the door simply isn't pushed fully shut, but a failed door switch, a sagged or misaligned strike, or a wiring fault can trigger dE even with the door apparently closed. Unlike heating or thermistor codes, dE fires before the cycle runs.

fixes.title: Close the door firmly
fixes.description: Push until it clicks. Check nothing (a dryer sheet, garment) is blocking the latch.

fixes.title: Test the door switch
fixes.description: Open the door and press the switch plunger — listen for a click. No click or no continuity = failed switch.

fixes.title: Check strike alignment
fixes.description: Confirm the door strike lines up with the latch; adjust if the door has sagged.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Close the door firmly until it clicks, then press Start. If dE persists with the door shut, test the switch.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If a new door switch doesn't clear dE, the control board door input has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dryers displaying the dE door code broadly, including electric and gas models across recent front-load-style dryer lines
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report dE appearing right at start-up, and it often clears with a firmer push until the latch clicks. A dryer sheet, stray garment, or lint wedged near the latch can falsely trigger it, and over time a door that has sagged may need the strike realigned before the code goes away.

error_codes.meaning: Thermistor error — drying temperature sensor out of range
error_codes.detail: tE3 fires when the control board reads a drying thermistor value outside its expected resistance window, indicating an open, shorted, or disconnected sensor circuit. Because the board can no longer trust the temperature feedback, it halts the cycle to avoid unsafe over- or under-heating. Unlike tE4, which flags a second temperature sensor, tE3 isolates the primary thermistor near the blower housing — its wiring, connector, or the sensor itself rather than a heater or airflow fault.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Unplug for 5 minutes — a transient sensor glitch may clear.

fixes.title: Reseat the thermistor connector
fixes.description: Locate the thermistor on the blower housing and reseat its connector.

fixes.title: Test and replace the thermistor
fixes.description: Check resistance against spec (varies by model). Out-of-range = replace.
fixes.warning: Unplug before accessing the blower housing.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug 5 minutes and restart. If tE3 returns, reseat then test the thermistor.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If a new thermistor and clean wiring don't resolve tE3, the control board sensor input has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG electric and gas dryers with electronic thermistor temperature control, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report tE3 appearing at startup or partway through a cycle, sometimes after the dryer has been moved or serviced, which can loosen the thermistor connector. A brief unplug occasionally clears a transient glitch, but many find reseating the connector or swapping the inexpensive thermistor is the lasting quick win.

error_codes.meaning: Thermistor error — secondary/outlet temperature sensor fault
error_codes.detail: tE4 fires when the control board reads the secondary (outlet) thermistor as out of spec or inconsistent with the inlet sensor. The board continuously compares both sensors; a resistance value beyond the expected range, or a mismatch that suggests one sensor is drifting, triggers this code and halts heating as a safety precaution. Unlike tE3, which points at the inlet sensor, tE4 implicates the outlet thermistor or the comparison between the two, distinguishing it from a straightforward single-sensor fault.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Unplug for 5 minutes to clear a transient fault.

fixes.title: Inspect both thermistors
fixes.description: Reseat connectors on the inlet and outlet thermistors; a mismatch between them can trigger tE4.

fixes.title: Test and replace the sensor
fixes.description: Measure resistance against spec and replace the out-of-range thermistor.
fixes.warning: Unplug before reaching into the blower housing.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug 5 minutes and restart. If tE4 returns, reseat and test both thermistors.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If both thermistors test good but tE4 persists, the control board sensor input has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG electric and gas dryers with dual thermistor temperature sensing, which covers most modern models including DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report tE4 appearing mid-cycle or shortly after startup, sometimes after the dryer has been moved or serviced, which can loosen a connector. A quick reseat of the outlet thermistor plug or a five-minute power cycle occasionally clears a transient trigger before any part replacement is needed.

error_codes.meaning: Humidity sensor error — moisture sensing failure
error_codes.detail: HS fires when the control board reads an out-of-range or unchanging signal from the moisture (humidity) sensor — the two metal bars inside the drum that measure conductivity across damp laundry. Because the board can no longer track dryness electrically, it disables Auto Dry logic and falls back to timed drying. Unlike a heating fault, HS doesn't stop the dryer or affect heat; it specifically flags the sensing circuit, which distinguishes it from thermistor or heater-related codes.

causes.description: Humidity sensor bars dirty (coated with fabric softener)

fixes.title: Clean the humidity sensor bars
fixes.description: The sensor is usually two metal bars inside the drum near the front. Clean with rubbing alcohol on a cotton ball. Fabric softener coating is the most common cause.

fixes.title: Test the sensor
fixes.description: Check resistance between the bars — should change when you hold them with wet fingers vs dry.

fixes.title: Replace the sensor
fixes.description: If cleaning doesn't resolve.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After cleaning sensor bars, run a test cycle on Auto Dry.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: HS rarely causes complete drying failure — dryer still runs, just can't auto-sense. Low urgency.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dryers equipped with electronic moisture sensing and Auto Dry cycles, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report HS appearing after heavy use of liquid fabric softener or dryer sheets, which leave an insulating film on the sensor bars. A quick wipe of the bars with rubbing alcohol often clears it, and loads that dry longer than expected are a frequent early hint before the code shows.

error_codes.meaning: Gas supply error — no gas detected (gas dryer models only)
error_codes.detail: On gas models, the control monitors for a confirmed flame during the ignition sequence. The igniter first glows to reach ignition temperature, then the gas valve coils open to release gas. If no flame is sensed within the allotted ignition attempts, the GAS code fires. Unlike a general heating fault, this code specifically points to the gas delivery or ignition chain — supply valve, igniter, or valve coils — rather than thermistors, thermostats, or the electric heating element.

fixes.title: Check the gas supply valve
fixes.description: Confirm the gas shutoff valve behind the dryer is fully open.

fixes.title: Check home gas supply
fixes.description: Confirm other gas appliances in the home are working.

fixes.title: Test the igniter
fixes.description: The igniter glows orange before the gas flame lights. If it glows but no flame, the gas valve coils have failed.
fixes.warning: Work with gas appliances only if qualified. If in doubt, call a technician.

fixes.title: Replace igniter if not glowing
fixes.description: No glow = igniter failure.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Restore gas supply and restart.
error_codes.models_affected: LG gas dryer models only, such as the DLG3601W; electric models (DLEX/DLE series) do not display this code
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Any gas dryer repair involving the gas valve, igniter, or burner assembly should be performed by a qualified technician if you are not experienced with gas appliances.
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report this appearing after moving the dryer or after gas service work, when the shutoff valve was left partly closed. A frequent quick win is simply confirming the valve behind the dryer is fully open and that other gas appliances still work; if the igniter glows but no flame appears, the gas valve coils are the usual suspect.

error_codes.meaning: High temperature error — drum temperature exceeded safe limit
error_codes.detail: The dH code fires when the control board reads drum or exhaust temperatures above the safe operating limit, typically via the thermistor working alongside the high-limit thermostat. Restricted airflow lets heat build up faster than it dissipates, tripping the safety threshold. Unlike the flow-check codes D90/D95 that flag restriction by monitoring dry time, dH reflects an actual measured overtemperature, and unlike a blown thermal fuse it usually still allows the drum to run until the fault registers.

fixes.title: Clean vent system thoroughly
fixes.description: Clean lint filter, full duct run, exterior cap.

fixes.title: Test high-limit thermostat
fixes.description: Test for continuity after cleaning. Tripped thermostat = replace.

fixes.title: Test thermal fuse
fixes.description: Blown fuse = no heat but motor runs.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After clearing vent and replacing failed components, unplug 5 minutes before restarting.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If vent is clear and thermostats are fine but overheating persists, heating element relay has stuck closed.
error_codes.models_affected: Most LG electric and gas dryers with electronic controls, including models such as DLEX3900W, DLE3500W, DLEX4500B, DLE7150W, and DLG3601W
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report dH appearing partway through a cycle, especially on heavy loads or after the vent hasn't been cleaned in a while. A false trigger can come from a crushed foil transition hose or a clogged exterior flap, so many find that a thorough vent cleaning clears it before any part replacement is needed.

error_codes.meaning: Power failure — cycle interrupted
error_codes.detail: The control board lost electrical power partway through a drying cycle and is notifying you that the cycle did not finish. Clothes may still be damp. This is a notice, not a fault — it's almost always a brief power outage or a tripped breaker on the dryer's circuit.

fixes.title: Restart cycle
fixes.description: Press Start to begin a new cycle.

fixes.title: Check circuit breaker
fixes.description: If breaker tripped, reset and investigate cause.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Press Start. PF clears automatically.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Not a fault. No service required.

error_codes.meaning: Leakage detected — water found in base tray
error_codes.detail: AE (also shown as E1) fires when the anti-flood float in the base tray rises, tripping a microswitch that tells the control board water has collected under the tub. The board then runs the drain pump and locks out further cycles to prevent flooding. Unlike OE, which flags a drain-out failure, AE specifically indicates water escaping into the base — from a leaking seal, hose, overfill, or cracked sump — rather than water failing to leave the tub.

causes.description: Loose water supply or drain hose connection
Overfill from stuck water inlet valve

fixes.title: Find and drain the base tray
fixes.description: Tilt the dishwasher slightly forward (with power off) to drain water from the base. Use towels.

fixes.title: Check the door seal
fixes.description: Run your hand along the entire door gasket. Replace if cracked, torn, or detached.

fixes.title: Check all hose connections
fixes.description: Inspect supply line, drain hose, and internal hose connections inside the door for looseness.

fixes.title: Inspect the tub
fixes.description: With the unit emptied, look for cracks in the plastic tub, especially at the sump area.

error_codes.reset_instructions: The AE code will clear automatically once the base tray is dry and the source of the leak is fixed. Run a short cycle to confirm no recurrence.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If no external leak source is found, the tub or pump housing may have a hairline crack requiring professional inspection.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dishwashers with a base-tray anti-flood float sensor (most full-size models, including LDFN4542S, LDTS5552S, and LDF5545ST)
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report AE appearing mid-cycle or at startup, sometimes after overfilling rinse aid, using too much or the wrong detergent (excess sudsing), or a one-time hose splash. A frequent quick win is drying the base tray fully and reseating a loose drain-hose connection; if it clears and doesn't return, the trigger was often minor.

error_codes.meaning: Drain error — dishwasher not draining
error_codes.detail: OE fires when the control board does not sense the tub water level dropping to empty within the allotted drain window, typically monitored by the pump run cycle and level sensor. It indicates a drainage failure rather than a fill or leak fault. Standing water remains in the sump because flow is blocked or the pump isn't moving water. This differs from AE (leak/overflow at the base pan) — OE means water won't leave, not that water is escaping.

causes.description: Drain filter clogged with food debris
Drain hose kinked or not high-looped
Garbage disposal knockout plug not removed

fixes.title: Clean the drain filter
fixes.description: Remove the bottom rack. Turn and lift out the filter assembly. Rinse under water, scrubbing debris with a soft brush. Reinstall firmly.

fixes.title: Check drain hose routing
fixes.description: The drain hose must have a high loop under the counter before connecting to the sink drain.

fixes.title: Check garbage disposal connection
fixes.description: If connected to a disposal, run the disposal before the dishwasher cycle.

fixes.title: Test the drain pump
fixes.description: If filter is clean and hose is clear, test the pump for operation.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After clearing the blockage, press Start to resume or restart cycle.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If the drain filter is clean and no drain hose obstruction exists, the pump needs professional testing.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dishwashers across most current front-control and top-control lines, including models such as LDFN4542S, LDTS5552S, and LDF5545ST
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report OE appearing at the end of a cycle with a pool of water left in the tub. A frequent quick win is simply pulling and rinsing the clogged drain filter, and on new installs the culprit is often the garbage disposal knockout plug never being removed.

error_codes.meaning: Water inlet error — not filling with water
error_codes.detail: The IE code fires when the control board detects that the tub has not reached the expected water level within the allotted fill time. A flow sensor or pressure/level switch monitors incoming water; if fill is too slow or absent, the cycle halts to protect the pump. This is distinct from an overfill or drain fault — IE points specifically to inadequate supply reaching the tub, whether from a closed valve, kinked hose, clogged inlet screen, or a failed inlet valve solenoid.

causes.description: Water supply valve under sink closed

fixes.title: Check the supply valve
fixes.description: Under the sink, confirm the dishwasher supply valve is fully open.

fixes.title: Check for kinks
fixes.description: Trace the supply hose from valve to dishwasher for kinks.

fixes.title: Clean the inlet screen
fixes.description: Disconnect the hose at the dishwasher inlet port and clean the mesh filter.

fixes.title: Replace inlet valve
fixes.description: If supply is confirmed good but no fill, replace the inlet valve.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Restore water supply and restart the cycle.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If supply is confirmed and still no fill, the inlet valve solenoid has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dishwashers across most recent generations, including models such as LDFN4542S, LDTS5552S, and LDF5545ST
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report IE showing up early in a cycle, often after the water was recently shut off for plumbing work and not fully reopened. A frequent quick win is simply confirming the under-sink supply valve is all the way open and straightening a hose kinked behind the unit; low household water pressure can also trigger it falsely.

error_codes.meaning: Water overflow — overfill protection triggered
error_codes.detail: FE fires when the control board sees water above the safe fill threshold, typically via the float switch or level sensing in the base. The most common trigger is an inlet valve that fails to fully close, letting water keep flowing after fill completes; the board then activates the drain pump to protect against leaks. FE differs from IE (fill too slow or no water) and AE (leak detected at the base pan) — FE specifically means too much water reached the tub.

causes.description: Float switch stuck in down position

fixes.title: Check inlet valve
fixes.description: Turn off water supply. If water stops entering, inlet valve is stuck open.

fixes.title: Check float switch
fixes.description: The float switch is in the base. Gently lift the float — it should move freely.

fixes.title: Replace inlet valve
fixes.description: If valve is confirmed stuck open.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Turn off the dishwasher's breaker for 60 seconds (most LG dishwashers are hardwired), then restore power. The unit should drain the excess water automatically; once empty, start a new cycle to confirm FE is cleared.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If inlet valve is replaced and overflow continues, the float switch or control board is faulty.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dishwashers across most lines, including models such as LDFN4542S, LDTS5552S, and LDF5545ST; the FE overflow code is a common feature of LG's built-in dishwashers.
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report FE appearing mid-cycle or shortly after a cycle starts, sometimes with the drain pump running continuously. A stuck or slow-closing inlet valve is a frequent culprit, though debris pinning the float down can cause a false trigger that clears after cleaning the base. Shutting the water supply to see if flow stops is a quick way many owners narrow it down.

error_codes.meaning: Heater failure — water not heating
error_codes.detail: The control board has detected that wash water is not reaching the target temperature — the heating element isn't producing heat. Dishes come out poorly cleaned and won't dry properly. An open-circuit heating element is the most common cause, followed by a failed temperature sensor.

fixes.title: Test heating element
fixes.description: Test continuity — 15–25 ohms expected.

fixes.title: Test NTC sensor
fixes.description: ~6k ohms at room temperature.

fixes.title: Replace failed component
fixes.description: Install appropriate part.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After repair, run full program.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If element and sensor good, control board relay failed.

error_codes.meaning: Temperature sensor failure
error_codes.detail: The tE code fires when the control board reads an out-of-range signal from the NTC thermistor monitoring wash-water temperature — either an open circuit (resistance too high) or a short (resistance too low). Because the board can't verify heat safely, it disables the heating element, leaving dishes washed in cold water with poor drying. This distinguishes tE from HE, which flags an actual heater circuit fault rather than a bad temperature reading. The thermistor or its connector is the usual culprit.

fixes.title: Test thermistor
fixes.description: ~6k–7k ohms at room temp.

fixes.title: Replace NTC sensor
fixes.description: Located near heating element.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Turn off the dishwasher's breaker for 60 seconds (LG dishwashers are typically hardwired), then restore power. After replacing the sensor, run a normal cycle and confirm the water heats without tE returning.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If new NTC and error persists, control board sensor input failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dishwashers with an NTC wash-water temperature sensor, which covers most modern models including LDFN4542S, LDTS5552S, and LDF5545ST
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly notice tE alongside cold dishes and weak drying rather than a hard stop mid-cycle. A loose or corroded sensor connector can falsely trigger it, so many report the code clearing after simply reseating the plug before buying a new sensor.

error_codes.meaning: Leakage detected — flood protection activated
error_codes.detail: Water has reached the base pan/flood sensor, so the dishwasher runs the drain pump and stops to prevent floor damage. The water is usually from a damaged door gasket, loose internal hose, or overfill from a sticking inlet valve rather than a bad control board.

fixes.title: Drain the base pan
fixes.description: Turn power off and carefully tilt the unit forward enough to drain trapped water from the base pan.
fixes.warning: Disconnect power first and protect the floor with towels.

fixes.title: Inspect the door seal
fixes.description: Check the gasket for tears, folds, or debris preventing a tight seal; clean or replace it if damaged.

fixes.title: Check hoses and clamps
fixes.description: Remove the kick plate and inspect fill, drain, and sump hose connections for drips during a short fill.

fixes.title: Test the inlet valve
fixes.description: If the tub overfills, replace a valve that continues feeding water after power is cut or the cycle stops.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After draining the base pan, do not just restart the dishwasher; dry the leak sensor area, fix the visible leak source, then run a short supervised cycle and stop immediately if water appears underneath.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Call a technician if LE returns but you cannot see a door, hose, or valve leak, because hidden sump seals, tub cracks, or wiring to the flood sensor require disassembly and leak testing under the machine.

error_codes.meaning: Motor/pump over-current error
error_codes.detail: CE indicates the control board has sensed the circulation pump motor drawing current beyond its safe threshold and cut power to protect the motor and electronics. It typically fires when the impeller is physically obstructed or when a motor winding shorts and overloads. Unlike OE, which points to a drainage or water-level problem, CE is strictly an over-current condition on the wash pump itself. The cycle halts with dishes left unwashed until the fault clears.

fixes.title: Check pump for debris
fixes.description: Remove filter and clear impeller area.
fixes.warning: Use gloves.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Unplug 5 minutes.

fixes.title: Replace circulation pump
fixes.description: If motor failed.

error_codes.reset_instructions: LG dishwashers are usually hardwired — turn off the breaker for 60 seconds instead of unplugging. After clearing any debris from the pump, restore power and run a short cycle to confirm CE is gone.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If pump replaced and CE persists, control board motor relay failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG dishwashers with an inverter-driven circulation pump, including models such as LDFN4542S, LDTS5552S, and LDF5545ST, plus most similar LG front-control units
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report CE appearing mid-cycle, often after a piece of glass, a fruit seed, or a shard of debris works into the pump area. Many find that clearing the filter and impeller then power-cycling at the breaker resolves it; if the code returns immediately, a failing pump or board relay is more likely.

error_codes.meaning: Freezer fan error — fan motor not operating
error_codes.detail: Er FF fires when the control board detects that the freezer evaporator fan motor is not spinning or is running below its target RPM, typically read from the fan's feedback signal. Because this fan pushes cold air off the evaporator coils into both compartments, a stall triggers the alarm quickly. It differs from Er CF, which points to the condenser fan; Er FF is specifically the internal evaporator fan behind the freezer back panel, often obstructed by frost.

causes.description: Fan blade broken or hitting ice/frost

fixes.title: Check for ice blockage
fixes.description: Remove the freezer back panel. Look for excessive frost or ice buildup around the fan blade. If present, manually defrost by leaving the refrigerator off for 24–48 hours.

fixes.title: Spin the fan blade
fixes.description: After defrosting, spin the fan blade by hand. It should rotate freely. If it's stiff or hitting something, clear the obstruction.

fixes.title: Test the fan motor
fixes.description: Disconnect the fan and apply 12VDC. If it doesn't spin with direct power, the motor has failed.

fixes.title: Replace the fan motor
fixes.description: The motor is mounted on a bracket behind the freezer back panel. Unscrew, disconnect, and install new motor.
fixes.warning: Unplug the refrigerator first.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After repair, plug in and allow 24 hours for temperatures to stabilize. The error code should clear automatically.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If ice buildup recurs within weeks after clearing, the defrost heater or defrost control is failing — this needs professional diagnosis.
error_codes.models_affected: LG refrigerators with a freezer evaporator fan monitored by the main control board, including models such as LFXS26973S, LFXS28968S, LRFVS3006S, LMXS28626S, and LRFDS3016S.
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report this code showing up after a defrost problem lets ice build up around the fan blade, and clearing the frost with a 24–48 hour defrost often restores normal operation. If the code returns within weeks, the underlying defrost system is a frequent culprit rather than the fan itself.

error_codes.meaning: Defrost heater error — defrost cycle not completing
error_codes.detail: The automatic defrost system has failed to complete a defrost cycle. Over time, frost will accumulate on the evaporator coils, eventually blocking airflow and causing the refrigerator to stop cooling. This is a common LG refrigerator issue often linked to a failed defrost heater or thermal limiter.

causes.description: Thermal fuse (limiter) blown on heater circuit

fixes.title: Manually defrost the coils
fixes.description: Turn off the refrigerator and allow all frost to melt. This provides temporary relief while you diagnose the root cause.

fixes.title: Access the evaporator and defrost heater
fixes.description: Remove the freezer back panel. The defrost heater wraps around or sits near the evaporator coils.

fixes.title: Test the heater for continuity
fixes.description: A working heater shows continuity (a few ohms). Open circuit = burned heater.

fixes.title: Test the thermal limiter
fixes.description: Small bi-metal devices on the heater circuit. Test each for continuity — a blown limiter shows open circuit.

fixes.title: Replace failed components
fixes.description: Install new heater, limiter(s), or thermostat as needed.
fixes.warning: Unplug refrigerator and allow ice to melt before accessing heater.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After repair, the defrost system will run on its regular schedule (every 8–12 hours). The Er dH code should clear after a successful defrost cycle.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If all defrost components test correctly, the main control board defrost control logic has failed.

error_codes.meaning: Condenser fan motor error
error_codes.detail: The control board monitors feedback (typically RPM or Hall-sensor pulses) from the condenser fan motor near the compressor. When it detects no rotation or an out-of-range speed signal, it logs Er CF. This differs from Er FF, which flags the evaporator (freezer) fan inside the cabinet. Common triggers are a seized bearing, physical obstruction from dust or pet hair, or broken wiring interrupting the feedback line, all of which prevent proper heat expulsion from the coils.

causes.description: Debris or pet hair blocking condenser fan

fixes.title: Clean the condenser coils and fan area
fixes.description: Access the back of the refrigerator and remove the access panel. Vacuum out all dust and pet hair from the fan blades and condenser coils.

fixes.title: Spin the condenser fan blade by hand
fixes.description: With power off, spin the fan blade. It should rotate freely. If stiff, the bearing has failed.

fixes.title: Replace the condenser fan motor
fixes.description: The motor is usually held by a single bracket. Disconnect wiring, unscrew bracket, and install new motor.
fixes.warning: Unplug before accessing the condenser area.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After repair, plug in and allow several hours for temperatures to stabilize. The code clears automatically.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If the fan is clean and free but the error persists, the control board fan relay may have failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG refrigerators using the Er error-code system, including bottom-freezer and French-door models such as the LFXS26973S, LFXS28968S, LRFVS3006S, LMXS28626S, and LRFDS3016S
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report this code appearing alongside weak cooling and a warmer-than-normal compressor area, often in homes with pets where hair packs the fan. A false trigger can occur when debris jams an otherwise good motor, so many find that a thorough cleaning and confirming the blade spins freely resolves it before any parts are needed.

error_codes.meaning: Freezer temperature sensor error
error_codes.detail: The control board is getting an out-of-range reading from the NTC thermistor that measures freezer compartment temperature. Without a valid reading the board runs the compressor on a fallback schedule, so the freezer may run too warm or too cold. An open-circuit thermistor is the usual cause.

fixes.title: Test thermistor
fixes.description: At 32°F/0°C: ~16k ohms.

fixes.title: Reseat connector
fixes.description: Firmly reconnect sensor wiring.

fixes.title: Replace thermistor
fixes.description: Clip-on replacement to evaporator.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Allow 24 hours temperature stabilization after repair.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If new thermistor and error persists, control board sensor input failed.

error_codes.meaning: Ice maker sensor failure
error_codes.detail: The control board is reading an open or shorted circuit from the ice maker temperature sensor. The board can't confirm the ice tray is cold enough to harvest, so ice production slows or stops entirely. The sensor itself usually fails, though heavy frost over the sensor can cause the same misread.

fixes.title: Defrost ice maker area
fixes.description: Fully thaw before diagnosing.

fixes.title: Test and replace sensor
fixes.description: Check resistance, replace if out of spec.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Press ice maker reset after repair.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If sensor replaced and error persists, ice maker control circuit failed.

error_codes.meaning: Ice maker fan error
error_codes.detail: Er IF fires when the control board receives no rotation feedback (Hall-sensor signal) from the ice maker compartment fan, which draws cold air across the ice mold. The board reads either a stalled motor or an absent tach pulse and halts ice production to avoid warming. Unlike Er FF, which targets the main freezer evaporator fan, Er IF is isolated to the ice room fan. Frost buildup jamming the blade mimics an outright motor failure, so defrosting first is essential.

fixes.title: Defrost ice maker
fixes.description: Allow complete defrost before fan diagnosis.

fixes.title: Test fan motor
fixes.description: Apply 12VDC — should spin freely.

fixes.title: Replace fan motor
fixes.description: Access through ice maker compartment.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug the refrigerator for 60 seconds to reset the board, then restore power. If ice was jamming the fan, leave the ice compartment door open to thaw first. Allow 24 hours for ice production to resume after repair.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If fan replaced and error persists, control board fan circuit failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG French-door and side-by-side refrigerators with a dedicated in-door or compartment ice maker, including models such as the LFXS26973S, LFXS28968S, LMXS28626S, LRFVS3006S, and LRFDS3016S
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report this code appearing after ice accumulates around the fan blade, and a full manual defrost with the compartment door left open often clears it without any parts. It can also be falsely triggered by a loose or frost-corroded fan connector, so checking the wiring before ordering a motor is worth a look.

error_codes.meaning: Communication error — main board to sub-board
error_codes.detail: The main control board has lost communication with the display/sub-board, so temperature settings, diagnostics, or ice/water controls may stop responding. A loose harness between the door/display board and the main board is most common; a failed sub-board or main board comes next.

causes.description: Wire harness connector loose between boards
Harness wire broken at door hinge

fixes.title: Power cycle the refrigerator
fixes.description: Unplug for 10 minutes so both boards reset, then restore power and check whether the display responds.

fixes.title: Reseat board connectors
fixes.description: Check and reseat the multi-pin connectors at the main board, display/sub-board, and door hinge harness.
fixes.warning: Unplug before accessing control boards.

fixes.title: Inspect the hinge harness
fixes.description: Look for pinched or broken wires where the door harness flexes; repair or replace any damaged harness.

fixes.title: Replace the sub-board first
fixes.description: If the wiring is sound, replace the cheaper display/sub-board before condemning the main control board.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug the refrigerator for 10 minutes, then restore power; if Er CO returns, reseat the display, door-hinge, and main-board connectors before replacing any board, because a loose harness is the most common fix.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Call a technician if Er CO persists after connectors are reseated, since isolating a broken door harness from a failed sub-board or main board requires live diagnostic checks and access to multiple control modules.

error_codes.meaning: Defrost sensor open circuit
error_codes.detail: The control board monitors the defrost thermistor clipped to the evaporator coil and expects a resistance within a defined range. When it reads an open circuit—no continuity—it posts dS. Without a valid reading the board cannot confirm the coil has warmed enough to end a defrost cycle, so frost accumulates and cooling weakens over time. This differs from Er dH, which points to the defrost heater circuit rather than the sensing thermistor itself.

fixes.title: Manually defrost
fixes.description: Unplug, allow complete defrost.

fixes.title: Test sensor
fixes.description: Room temp: ~6k–8k ohms. At 32°F: ~16k ohms.

fixes.title: Replace sensor
fixes.description: Unclip from evaporator and replace.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Allow 24 hours for normal operation after repair.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If defrost sensor is fine and dS persists, defrost heater or control board defrost circuit has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG refrigerators with a defrost sensor mounted on the evaporator, including French-door and side-by-side models such as the LFXS26973S, LFXS28968S, LRFVS3006S, LMXS28626S, and LRFDS3016S
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report dS appearing alongside a gradual loss of cooling or noticeable frost buildup on the back panel of the fresh-food or freezer compartment. A loose or corroded sensor connector can occasionally trigger a false reading, so reseating the harness before replacing the part is a frequent quick win.

error_codes.meaning: Refrigerator (fresh-food) compartment sensor error
error_codes.detail: Er rS fires when the control board sees the fresh-food compartment thermistor as an open or shorted circuit rather than a plausible resistance. This distinguishes it from Er FS (freezer sensor) and Er IS (ice-maker sensor), which flag other thermistors on separate inputs. Losing a valid reading forces the board onto a fallback cooling cycle, so the fridge section drifts warm or cold. A failed sensor is most common, followed by corroded or loose connectors in the damp cabinet.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Unplug for 5 minutes; a transient sensor misread may clear.

fixes.title: Inspect and reseat the connector
fixes.description: Locate the fresh-food sensor connector and check for corrosion or a loose fit.
fixes.warning: Unplug before opening interior panels.

fixes.title: Test and replace the sensor
fixes.description: Compare resistance to spec (typically several kΩ near room temperature). Replace if out of range.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug for 5 minutes. If Er rS returns, test the fresh-food sensor and reseat its connector before replacing.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If the sensor reads in spec and the harness is sound but Er rS persists, the control board's sensor input has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG refrigerators that report the Er rS code, spanning French-door and side-by-side models such as the LFXS26973S, LFXS28968S, LRFVS3006S, LMXS28626S, and LRFDS3016S, as well as similar LG units with a dedicated fresh-food thermistor.
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report Er rS appearing alongside uneven fresh-food temperatures, sometimes after a humid spell or a defrost cycle that leaves moisture around the connector. A brief unplug occasionally clears a transient misread, but many find reseating a corroded connector or swapping the inexpensive sensor is the lasting quick win.

error_codes.meaning: Humidity sensor error
error_codes.detail: Er HS fires when the control board reads the humidity sensor's signal as open, shorted, or outside its valid resistance/voltage range, so the board can no longer trust readings used to drive anti-condensation (mullion) heaters and damper airflow. It then falls back to default humidity logic. Unlike Er rS, which points to a temperature/thermistor sensor fault, Er HS is specific to the humidity-sensing input, most often a failed sensor or a loose or corroded connector rather than a compartment cooling problem.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Unplug for 5 minutes to clear a transient reading.

fixes.title: Reseat the sensor connector
fixes.description: Locate the humidity sensor (near the fresh-food section/control housing) and reseat its connector.
fixes.warning: Unplug before accessing interior wiring.

fixes.title: Replace the humidity sensor
fixes.description: If reseating doesn't help, replace the sensor.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Unplug for 5 minutes. If Er HS returns, reseat then replace the humidity sensor.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If a new humidity sensor doesn't clear Er HS, the control board's sensor circuit has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG French-door and multi-door refrigerators equipped with a humidity sensor for anti-condensation heater and damper control; not all models carry this sensor
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report this code appearing alongside condensation or sweating around the door seams or mullion, and it sometimes clears on its own after a power cycle if the reading was a transient glitch. A quick reseat of the sensor connector reportedly resolves many cases before a sensor swap is needed.

error_codes.meaning: Flow sensor error — low water pressure or flow meter fault
error_codes.detail: Er gF flags the water flow sensor (flow meter) on the dispenser/ice-maker line. The board uses it to measure how much water flows during a fill; if it sees no flow or an implausible count, it raises gF. Low household water pressure, a closed or kinked supply, or a clogged inlet are common causes, alongside a failed flow sensor. If the ice maker is overfilling, a stuck flow meter is the prime suspect.

causes.description: Low house water pressure or supply valve not fully open
Kinked or frozen water supply line

fixes.title: Check the water supply
fixes.description: Confirm the saddle/supply valve is fully open and the line behind the fridge isn't kinked or frozen.

fixes.title: Replace a clogged water filter
fixes.description: A heavily clogged filter restricts flow enough to trigger gF; install a fresh OEM filter.

fixes.title: Inspect the inlet valve and flow sensor
fixes.description: If supply and filter are good, test the inlet valve and the flow meter for proper operation.
fixes.warning: Unplug and shut off water before servicing the valve.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Confirm the supply valve is fully open and the filter is fresh, then power cycle. If gF persists, test the inlet valve and flow sensor.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If water pressure, filter, and inlet valve are confirmed good but Er gF persists, the flow sensor or its control board input has failed.

error_codes.meaning: Heat error — oven failed to reach temperature in time
error_codes.detail: F11 is a preheat timeout: the control monitors the temperature sensor during startup and expects the cavity to climb toward roughly 150°F within about five minutes. If that rate isn't met, it assumes the heat source or feedback path is faulty and aborts. Unlike sensor-specific faults, F11 flags the outcome (no heat rise) rather than a shorted or open sensor reading, so a weak gas igniter, dead element, or a control that never energized the circuit can all trigger it.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Turn off the breaker for 5 minutes. A transient F11 from a one-time slow ignition may clear.

fixes.title: Test the temperature sensor
fixes.description: At room temperature the sensor should read ~1,080 ohms. Open or out-of-spec = replace.

fixes.title: Inspect the igniter (gas)
fixes.description: Watch ignition — a glow igniter that's dim/orange and slow to light the burner is failing.
fixes.warning: Allow oven to cool and shut off gas before servicing the igniter.

fixes.title: Inspect the element (electric)
fixes.description: Check the bake element for breaks or no continuity.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Power off at the breaker for 5 minutes and retry a bake cycle. If F11 returns during preheat, test the sensor/igniter.
error_codes.models_affected: LG electric ranges such as the LREL6325F and gas ranges such as the LRGL5825F, plus related LRE/LSE and LRG/LSG series
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If the sensor and igniter/element all test good but F11 persists, the control board has failed.
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report F11 appearing a few minutes into preheat, sometimes after the oven has been sluggish to heat for a while. A single slow ignition can throw a one-off code that clears on a power cycle, but a code that returns each cycle usually points to a genuinely weak igniter or failed element rather than a false trigger.

error_codes.meaning: Temperature sensor error — sensor or wiring fault
error_codes.detail: F19 fires when the control board reads the oven's RTD temperature sensor outside its valid resistance window — effectively shorted (near 0 ohms), open (OL), or intermittent from a damaged harness. Because a safe bake requires reliable feedback, the control locks out heating until the circuit reads correctly. On some LG models a stuck control-panel key can also trigger F19. This distinguishes it from calibration or heating-element faults; F19 points specifically at the sensor input path.

fixes.title: Power cycle
fixes.description: Turn off the breaker for 5 minutes, then retry.

fixes.title: Check for a stuck key
fixes.description: Press each control-panel button to confirm none is sticking.

fixes.title: Test the sensor
fixes.description: At room temperature, ~1,080 ohms. Near 0 = shorted, OL = open. Replace if out of spec.
fixes.warning: Allow oven to cool before reaching inside.

fixes.title: Inspect the wiring
fixes.description: Trace the sensor harness for pinch points or chafing against the cavity.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Power off 5 minutes, then test the sensor. After replacing it, confirm with a 350°F bake cycle.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If a new sensor and clean wiring don't resolve F19, the control board sensor input has failed.
error_codes.models_affected: LG electric and gas ovens/ranges with an RTD temperature sensor, including models such as LREL6325F and LRGL5825F
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report F19 appearing at power-up or partway through a bake, sometimes after a self-clean cycle stresses the wiring. A simple power cycle occasionally clears a one-off glitch, and checking for a stuck button is a frequent quick win before pulling the sensor.

error_codes.meaning: Hot Surface indicator — surface still hot (not a fault)
error_codes.detail: HS is an informational indicator, not an error. It warns that a cooking surface or the oven cavity is still hot enough to cause burns. It clears on its own once the surface cools.

causes.description: Surface still above the hot-to-touch threshold

fixes.title: Allow the surface to cool
fixes.description: Wait for the indicator to clear — keep hands and children away until it does.

error_codes.reset_instructions: No action needed. HS clears automatically once the surface cools.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: HS is a safety indicator, not a fault. No service required.

error_codes.meaning: Power failure — unit lost power during operation (not a fault)
error_codes.detail: PF is the control board's record that supply voltage was interrupted mid-operation, then restored. When power returns, the board detects that a cooking or clock program was running and posts PF to flag the interruption rather than a component fault. It differs from sensor or communication codes (like Sb) because no hardware is failing — the board is simply reporting an unstable or momentarily lost power feed to the terminal block.

fixes.title: Clear the code
fixes.description: Press Clear/Off (or Cancel) to dismiss PF and resume normal use.

fixes.title: Check the breaker
fixes.description: If PF recurs, confirm the range's breaker isn't tripping and the terminal-block connections are tight.
fixes.warning: Unplug or shut off the breaker before checking the terminal block.

error_codes.reset_instructions: Press Clear/Off. PF clears once power is stable.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: PF is normally not a fault. Call a pro only if it recurs without any outage — a wiring or board issue may be present.
error_codes.models_affected: LG electric and gas ranges with a digital display, including models such as LREL6325F and LRGL5825F
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report PF after a storm, a brief outage, or a breaker trip, and it typically clears with a single press of Clear/Off. If it keeps returning without any obvious outage, people often trace it to a loose terminal-block connection or a weak breaker.

error_codes.meaning: Sabbath Mode active — controls intentionally disabled (not a fault)
error_codes.detail: Sb indicates Sabbath Mode is turned on. In this mode the oven keeps a set bake temperature with the display, tones, and most controls disabled for religious observance. It is not an error. Note: after a power failure the display returns in Sabbath mode with the oven off.

fixes.title: Exit Sabbath Mode
fixes.description: Press and hold the Sabbath/Clock controls per your model's manual until Sb clears (typically a 3-second hold).

error_codes.reset_instructions: Hold the Sabbath/Clock button combination to exit. Sb is not a fault.
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: Sb is a feature, not a fault. No service required.

error_codes.meaning: Oven temperature sensor open circuit
error_codes.detail: F1 fires when the control board reads an open circuit on the oven's resistance temperature detector (RTD), meaning the sensor loop shows infinite resistance instead of the roughly 1,080 ohms expected at room temperature. Because safe bake and broil control depends on continuous temperature feedback, the board locks out heating. This differs from shorted-sensor or out-of-range readings flagged by related codes, where resistance is measurable but incorrect rather than completely broken.

fixes.title: Test the sensor
fixes.description: Disconnect the sensor and test resistance. LG oven sensors read approximately 1,080 ohms at room temperature (75°F). An open circuit (OL/∞) means sensor failure.

fixes.title: Check the connector
fixes.description: Reseat the connector at both the sensor and the control board.

fixes.title: Replace the sensor
fixes.description: The sensor is in the upper-back of the oven cavity, secured with 1–2 screws.
fixes.warning: Allow oven to cool completely before reaching inside.

error_codes.reset_instructions: After replacing sensor, turn on and test with a 350°F bake cycle.
error_codes.models_affected: LG electric and gas ranges/ovens using an RTD temperature sensor, including models such as LREL6325F and LRGL5825F
error_codes.when_to_call_pro: If new sensor doesn't resolve F1, the control board temperature input has failed.
error_codes.real_world_notes: Owners commonly report F1 appearing at startup or partway through a bake cycle, sometimes with the oven refusing to heat at all. A loose or corroded sensor connector can trigger a false alarm, and many report that simply reseating the connector or swapping the inexpensive sensor clears the code.

error_codes.meaning: Oven over-temperature — exceeded maximum safe temperature
error_codes.detail: The F2 code fires when the control board reads oven cavity temperature above its safe ceiling, generally around 590°F in normal cooking, and cuts power to protect the appliance. It differs from open- or short-sensor faults (often flagged as F1/F3) because the sensor is reporting a genuinely high value or a stuck heating-element relay is feeding the element continuously. The board can't distinguish a drifting sensor from real overheating, so it treats any over-limit reading as a critical shutdown.

causes.description: Temperature sensor failure causing incorrect readings
